([Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"): غزنویان, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Persian "Romanization of Persian"): *Ġaznaviyān*) was a culturally [Persianate](/wiki/Persianate "Persianate"),[[b]](#cite_note-b-10) [Sunni Muslim](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam") state of [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_peoples "Turkic peoples")[[9]](#cite_note-11) *[mamluk](/wiki/Mamluk "Mamluk")* origin. Flourishing from 977 to 1186, the empire spread from the [Oxus](/wiki/Oxus "Oxus") to the [Indus Valley](/wiki/Indus_Valley "Indus Valley") at its greatest extent. The dynasty was founded by [Sabuktigin](/wiki/Sabuktigin "Sabuktigin") upon his succession to the rule of [Ghazna](/wiki/Ghazni_Province "Ghazni Province") after the death of his father-in-law, [Alp Tigin](/wiki/Alp_Tigin "Alp Tigin"), who was an ex-general of the [Samanid Empire](/wiki/Samanid_Empire "Samanid Empire") from [Balkh](/wiki/Balkh "Balkh").

The **Ghaznavid Empire** ([Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"): غزنویان, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Persian "Romanization of Persian"): *Ġaznaviyān*) was a culturally [Persianate](/wiki/Persianate "Persianate"),[[b]](#cite_note-b-10) [Sunni Muslim](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam") state of [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_peoples "Turkic peoples")[[9]](#cite_note-11) *[mamluk](/wiki/Mamluk "Mamluk")* origin. Flourishing from 977 to 1186, the empire spread from the [Oxus](/wiki/Oxus "Oxus") to the [Indus Valley](/wiki/Indus_Valley "Indus Valley") at its greatest extent. The dynasty was founded by [Sabuktigin](/wiki/Sabuktigin "Sabuktigin") upon his succession to the rule of [Ghazna](/wiki/Ghazni_Province "Ghazni Province") after the death of his father-in-law, [Alp Tigin](/wiki/Alp_Tigin "Alp Tigin"), who was an ex-general of the [Samanid Empire](/wiki/Samanid_Empire "Samanid Empire") from [Balkh](/wiki/Balkh "Balkh").

Sabuktigin's son, [Mahmud of Ghazni](/wiki/Mahmud_of_Ghazni "Mahmud of Ghazni"), expanded the Ghaznavid Empire to the [Amu Darya](/wiki/Amu_Darya "Amu Darya"), the [Indus River](/wiki/Indus_River "Indus River") and the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean") in the east and to [Rey](/wiki/Ray,_Iran "Ray, Iran") and [Hamadan](/wiki/Hamadan "Hamadan") in the west. Under the reign of [Mas'ud I](/wiki/Mas%CA%BDud_I "Masʽud I"), the Ghaznavid dynasty began losing control over its western territories to the [Seljuk Empire](/wiki/Seljuk_Empire "Seljuk Empire") after the [Battle of Dandanaqan](/wiki/Battle_of_Dandanaqan "Battle of Dandanaqan") in 1040, resulting in a restriction of its holdings to modern-day Afghanistan, Pakistan and Northern India.

In 1151, Sultan Bahram Shah lost Ghazni to the [Ghurid](/wiki/Ghurid_dynasty "Ghurid dynasty") sultan [Ala al-Din Husayn](/wiki/Ala_al-Din_Husayn "Ala al-Din Husayn"). The Ghaznavids retook Ghazni, but lost the city to the [Ghuzz Turks](/wiki/Oghuz_Turks "Oghuz Turks") who in turn lost it to [Muhammad of Ghor](/wiki/Muhammad_of_Ghor "Muhammad of Ghor"). In response, the Ghaznavids fled to Lahore, their regional capital. In 1186, [Lahore was conquered](/wiki/Siege_of_Lahore_(1186) "Siege of Lahore (1186)") by the Ghurid sultan, Muhammad of Ghor, with its Ghaznavid ruler, [Khusrau Malik](/wiki/Khusrau_Malik "Khusrau Malik"), imprisoned and later executed.

## Rise to power

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Portrait_from_the_Palace_courtroom%2C_Lashkari_Bazar.jpg/250px-Portrait_from_the_Palace_courtroom%2C_Lashkari_Bazar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_from_the_Palace_courtroom,_Lashkari_Bazar.jpg)

Ghaznavid portrait, Palace of [Lashkari Bazar](/wiki/Lashkari_Bazar "Lashkari Bazar"). [Schlumberger](/wiki/Daniel_Schlumberger "Daniel Schlumberger") noted that the [turban](/wiki/Turban "Turban"), the small mouth and the strongly slanted eyes were characteristically Turkic.[[10]](#cite_note-12) 11th century

Two military families arose from the Turkic slave-guards of the [Samanid Empire](/wiki/Samanid_Empire "Samanid Empire"), the [Simjurids](/wiki/Simjurids "Simjurids") and Ghaznavids, who ultimately proved disastrous to the Samanids. The Simjurids received an [appanage](/wiki/Appanage "Appanage") in the [Kohistan](/wiki/Quhistan "Quhistan") region of eastern [Khorasan](/wiki/Khorasan "Khorasan"). The Samanid generals Alp Tigin and [Abu al-Hasan Simjuri](/wiki/Abu_al-Hasan_Simjuri "Abu al-Hasan Simjuri") competed for the governorship of Khorasan and control of the Samanid Empire by placing on the throne [emirs](/wiki/Emir "Emir") they could dominate after the death of [Abd al-Malik I](/wiki/Abd_al-Malik_I_(Samanid_emir) "Abd al-Malik I (Samanid emir)") in 961. His death created a succession crisis between his brothers.

A court party instigated by men of the scribal class – civilian ministers rather than Turkic generals – rejected the candidacy of Alp Tigin for the Samanid throne. [Mansur I](/wiki/Mansur_I "Mansur I") was installed instead, and Alp Tigin prudently retired to south of the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush"), where he captured Ghazna and became the ruler of the city as a Samanid authority.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth2006-13) The Simjurids enjoyed control of Khorasan south of the [Amu Darya](/wiki/Amu_Darya "Amu Darya") but were hard-pressed by a third great Iranian dynasty, the [Buyid dynasty](/wiki/Buyid_dynasty "Buyid dynasty"), and were unable to survive the collapse of the Samanids and the subsequent rise of the Ghaznavids.

The struggles of the Turkic slave generals for mastery of the throne with the help of shifting allegiance from the court's ministerial leaders both demonstrated and accelerated the Samanid decline. Samanid weakness attracted into Transoxiana the [Karluks](/wiki/Karluks "Karluks"), a Turkic people who had recently converted to Islam. They occupied [Bukhara](/wiki/Bukhara "Bukhara") in 992, establishing in Transoxania the [Kara-Khanid Khanate](/wiki/Kara-Khanid_Khanate "Kara-Khanid Khanate").[[12]](#cite_note-14)

Alp Tigin's died in 963, and after two ghulam governors and three years, his slave [Sabuktigin](/wiki/Sabuktigin "Sabuktigin") became the governor of Ghazna.

## Domination

### Sabuktigin

Main article: [Sabuktigin](/wiki/Sabuktigin "Sabuktigin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Ghaznavid_ruins_of_Lashkari_Bazar_%28northern_view%2C_composite%29.jpg/500px-Ghaznavid_ruins_of_Lashkari_Bazar_%28northern_view%2C_composite%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ghaznavid_ruins_of_Lashkari_Bazar_(northern_view,_composite).jpg)

Ghaznavid fortress of [Lashkari Bazar](/wiki/Lashkari_Bazar "Lashkari Bazar") in [Lashkargah](/wiki/Lashkargah "Lashkargah"), ancient Bost, southern [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"). It was founded by [Mahmud of Ghazni](/wiki/Mahmud_of_Ghazni "Mahmud of Ghazni") in 998–1030 CE.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/5/56/Lashkari_Bazar_guard_drawing.jpg/120px-Lashkari_Bazar_guard_drawing.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lashkari_Bazar_guard_drawing.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Ghaznavid_figures_in_the_wall_paintings_from_one_of_the_Ghaznavid_palaces_at_La%C5%A1kar%C4%AB_B%C4%81z%C4%81r_in_central_Afghanistan%2C_probably_built_by_Masud_I_%281030-41%29.jpg/250px-Ghaznavid_figures_in_the_wall_paintings_from_one_of_the_Ghaznavid_palaces_at_La%C5%A1kar%C4%AB_B%C4%81z%C4%81r_in_central_Afghanistan%2C_probably_built_by_Masud_I_%281030-41%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ghaznavid_figures_in_the_wall_paintings_from_one_of_the_Ghaznavid_palaces_at_La%C5%A1kar%C4%AB_B%C4%81z%C4%81r_in_central_Afghanistan,_probably_built_by_Masud_I_(1030-41).jpg)

Figures in the wall paintings from the Ghaznavid palace of [Lashkari Bazar](/wiki/Lashkari_Bazar "Lashkari Bazar"), probably dating to the period of [Mahmud of Ghazni](/wiki/Mahmud_of_Ghazni "Mahmud of Ghazni").[[13]](#cite_note-15) Black-and-white [line drawing](/wiki/Line_art "Line art") of the left figure, by the discoverer [Daniel Schlumberger](/wiki/Daniel_Schlumberger "Daniel Schlumberger") (1978).[[14]](#cite_note-16) The figures wear the typical Turkic attire.[[15]](#cite_note-17)

Sabuktigin lived as a [mamluk](/wiki/Mamluk "Mamluk"), Turkic slave-soldier,[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeviSela201083-18)[[c]](#cite_note-19)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth19634-20) during his youth and later married the daughter of his master [Alptigin](/wiki/Alp_Tigin "Alp Tigin"),[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAsherTalbot200619-21) who fled to [Ghazna](/wiki/Ghazni "Ghazni") following a failed coup attempt, and conquered the city from the local [Lawik rulers](/wiki/Lawik_dynasty "Lawik dynasty") in 962.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth196337-22) After Alptigin death, his son Abu Ishaq Ibrahim governed Ghazna for three years.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth196338-23) His death was followed by the reign of a former ghulam of Alptigin, Bilgetigin. Bilgetigin's rule was so harsh the populace invited [Abu Bakr Lawik](/wiki/Abu_Bakr_Lawik "Abu Bakr Lawik") back.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth196338-23) It was through Sabuktigin's military ability that Lawik was removed, Bilgetigin was exiled, and Sabuktigin gained the governorship.[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth196339-24)

Once established as governor of Ghazna, Sabuktigin was asked to intervene in Khurasan, at the insistence of the Samanid emir, and after a victorious campaign received the governorships of Balkh, Tukharistan, Bamiyan, Ghur and Gharchistan.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth196344-25) Sabuktigin inherited a governorship in turmoil.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth196342-26) In Zabulistan, the typical military fief system(*mustaghall*) were being changed into permanent ownership(*tamlik*) which resulted in the Turkic soldiery unwilling to take up arms.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth196342-26) Sabuktigin reformed the system making them all a *mustaghall*-type fief.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth196342-26) In 976, he ended the conflict between two Turkic ghulams at Bust and restored the original ruler.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth1994203-27) Later that same year, Sabuktigin campaigned against Qusdar, catching the ruler(possibly Mu'tazz b. Ahmad) off guard and obtaining an annual tribute from him.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth1994203-27)

After the death of Sabuktigin, his son by Alptigin's daughter, [Ismail](/wiki/Ismail_of_Ghazni "Ismail of Ghazni"), was given Ghazna.[[d]](#cite_note-29)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth196345-30) Another son, Abu'l-Muzaffar Nasr, was given the governorship of Bust, while in Khorasan, the eldest son Mahmud, was given command of the army.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth196344-25) Sabuktigin's intent was to ensure governorships for his family, despite the decaying influence of the Samanid Empire, and did not consider his dynasty as independent.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth196344-25) Ismail, upon gaining his inheritance, quickly traveled to Bust and did homage to Emir Abu'l-Harith Mansur b. Nuh.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth196345-30) Mahmud, who had been left out of any significant inheritance, proposed a division of power, to which Ismail refused.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth1975169-31) Mahmud marched on Ghazna and subsequently Ismail was defeated and captured in 998 at the [Battle of Ghazni](/wiki/Battle_of_Ghazni_(998) "Battle of Ghazni (998)").[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoy201588-28)

### Mahmud, son of Sabuktigin

Main article: [Mahmud of Ghazni](/wiki/Mahmud_of_Ghazni "Mahmud of Ghazni")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Mahmud_of_Ghazni%2C_Ghaznavid_ruler%2C_conquering_Qasdar_%28modern_Khuzdar%29_in_India%2C_miniature_from_the_Jami%CA%BF_al-Tawarikh_of_Rashid_al-Din_Il-Khanid_Tabriz_Ms_Or_20_f.108v.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mahmud_of_Ghazni,_Ghaznavid_ruler,_conquering_Qasdar_(modern_Khuzdar)_in_India,_miniature_from_the_Jami%CA%BF_al-Tawarikh_of_Rashid_al-Din_Il-Khanid_Tabriz_Ms_Or_20_f.108v.jpg)

Mahmud of Ghazni. *[Jamiʿ al-Tawarikh](/wiki/Jami%CA%BF_al-Tawarikh "Jamiʿ al-Tawarikh")*, 1314-15

In 998, [Mahmud](/wiki/Mahmud_of_Ghaznavid "Mahmud of Ghaznavid"), son of Sebuktigin, succeeded to the governorship, and Ghazni and the Ghaznavid dynasty became perpetually associated with him. He emphasized his loyalty in a letter to the caliph, saying that the Samanids had only been replaced because of their treason.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy1986301-32) Mahmud received the governorship of Khurasan and titles of Yamin al-Dawla and Amin al-Milla.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy1986301-32) As a representative of caliphal authority, he championed Sunni Islam by campaigning against the Ismaili and Shi'ite Buyids.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy1986301-32) He completed the conquest of the Samanid and [Shahi](/wiki/Hindu_Shahi "Hindu Shahi") territories, including the [Ismaili](/wiki/Ismaili "Ismaili") [Kingdom of Multan](/wiki/Emirate_of_Multan "Emirate of Multan"), [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh"), as well as some [Buwayhid](/wiki/Buyid_dynasty "Buyid dynasty") territory.

By all accounts, the rule of Mahmud was the golden age and height of the Ghaznavid Empire. Mahmud carried out seventeen expeditions through northern India to establish his control and set up tributary states, and his raids also resulted in the looting of a great deal of plunder. He established his authority from the borders of [Ray](/wiki/Ray,_Iran "Ray, Iran") to [Samarkand](/wiki/Samarkand "Samarkand"), from the [Caspian Sea](/wiki/Caspian_Sea "Caspian Sea") to the [Yamuna](/wiki/Yamuna "Yamuna").

During Mahmud's reign (997–1030), the Ghaznavids settled 4,000 [Turkmen](/wiki/Turkmen_people "Turkmen people") families near Farana in Khorasan. By 1027, due to the Turkmen raiding neighbouring settlements, the governor of Tus, Abu l'Alarith Arslan Jadhib, led military strikes against them. The Turkmen were defeated and scattered to neighbouring lands.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth1963224-33) Still, as late as 1033, Ghaznavid governor [Tash Farrash](/wiki/Tash_Farrash "Tash Farrash") executed fifty Turkmen chiefs for raids into Khorasan.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth1963225-34)

#### Indian conquests

Main article: [Ghaznavid campaigns in India](/wiki/Ghaznavid_campaigns_in_India "Ghaznavid campaigns in India")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Mahmud_ibn_Sebuktegin%2C_Ghaznavid_ruler%2C_conquering_Qasdar_%28modern_Khuzdar%29_in_India%2C_miniature_from_the_Jami%CA%BF_al-Tawarikh_of_Rashid_al-Din_Il-Khanid_Tabriz_Ms_Or_20_f.108v.jpg/500px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mahmud_ibn_Sebuktegin,_Ghaznavid_ruler,_conquering_Qasdar_(modern_Khuzdar)_in_India,_miniature_from_the_Jami%CA%BF_al-Tawarikh_of_Rashid_al-Din_Il-Khanid_Tabriz_Ms_Or_20_f.108v.jpg)

Mahmud of Ghazni conquering Qasdar (modern [Khuzdar](/wiki/Khuzdar "Khuzdar")) in India. *[Jamiʿ al-Tawarikh](/wiki/Jami%CA%BF_al-Tawarikh "Jamiʿ al-Tawarikh")*

Mahmud of Ghazni led incursions deep into [India](/wiki/India "India"), as far as [Mathura](/wiki/Mathura "Mathura"), [Kannauj](/wiki/Kannauj "Kannauj") and [Somnath](/wiki/Somnath "Somnath"). In 1001, he defeated the [Hindu Shahi](/wiki/Hindu_Shahi "Hindu Shahi") in the [Battle of Peshawar](/wiki/Battle_of_Peshawar_(1001) "Battle of Peshawar (1001)"). In 1004-5, he invaded the [Principality of Bhatiya](/wiki/Bhera "Bhera") and in 1006 the neighbouring [Emirate of Multan](/wiki/Emirate_of_Multan "Emirate of Multan").[[31]](#cite_note-DAA-35) In 1008-9, he again vanquished the [Hindu Shahis](/wiki/Hindu_Shahis "Hindu Shahis") at the [Battle of Chach](/wiki/Battle_of_Chach "Battle of Chach"), and established Governors in the conquered areas.[[31]](#cite_note-DAA-35) In India, the Ghaznavids were called *Turushkas* ("Turks") or *Hammiras* (from the Arabic *Amir* "Commander").[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEaton201929-36)

In 1018, he laid waste the city of [Mathura](/wiki/Mathura "Mathura"), which was "ruthlessly sacked, ravaged, desecrated and destroyed".[[33]](#cite_note-37)[[34]](#cite_note-38) According to [Muhammad Qasim Hindu Shah](/wiki/Firishta "Firishta"), writing an "History of Hindustan" in the 16th-17th century, the city of Mathura was the richest in India. When it was attacked by Mahmud of Ghazni, ["all the idols" were burnt and destroyed](/wiki/Art_of_Mathura "Art of Mathura") during a period of twenty days, gold and silver was smelted for booty, and the city was burnt down.[[35]](#cite_note-39) In 1018 Mahmud also captured [Kanauj](/wiki/Kanauj "Kanauj"), the capital of the [Pratiharas](/wiki/Pratihara_dynasty "Pratihara dynasty"), and then confronted the [Chandelas](/wiki/Chandelas_of_Jejakabhukti "Chandelas of Jejakabhukti"), from whom he obtained the payment of tribute.[[36]](#cite_note-40) In 1026, he raided and plundered the [Somnath temple](/wiki/Somnath_temple "Somnath temple"), taking away a booty of 20 million dinars.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYagnikSheth200539–40-41)[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThapar200436–37-42)

The wealth brought back from Mahmud's [Indian](/wiki/India "India") expeditions to Ghazni was enormous, and contemporary historians (*e.g.*, [Abolfazl Beyhaghi](/wiki/Abolfazl_Beyhaghi "Abolfazl Beyhaghi"), [Ferdowsi](/wiki/Ferdowsi "Ferdowsi")) give glowing descriptions of the magnificence of the capital and of the conqueror's munificent support of literature.[[39]](#cite_note-43) Mahmud died in April 1030 and had chosen his son, Mohammed, as his successor.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth1963228-44)

## Decline

### Twin sons of Mahmud

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Dado_panel_with_club-bearer_and_abraded_inscription%2C_possibly_from_Rawza%2C_Ghazni_%28National_Museum_of_Afghanistan_Kabul%2C_1958%2C_inv._no._KM58.2.X%29.jpg/250px-Dado_panel_with_club-bearer_and_abraded_inscription%2C_possibly_from_Rawza%2C_Ghazni_%28National_Museum_of_Afghanistan_Kabul%2C_1958%2C_inv._no._KM58.2.X%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dado_panel_with_club-bearer_and_abraded_inscription,_possibly_from_Rawza,_Ghazni_(National_Museum_of_Afghanistan_Kabul,_1958,_inv._no._KM58.2.X).jpg)

Dado panel with club-bearer and abraded inscription, possibly from Rawza, Ghazni. 11th-12th century (National Museum of Afghanistan Kabul, 1958, inv. no. KM58.2.X).

Mahmud left the empire to his son Mohammed, who was mild, affectionate and soft. His brother, [Mas'ud](/wiki/Mas%27ud_I_of_Ghazni "Mas'ud I of Ghazni"), asked for three provinces that he had won by his sword, but his brother did not consent. Mas'ud had to fight his brother, and he became king, blinding and imprisoning Mohammed as punishment. Mas'ud was unable to preserve the empire and following a disastrous defeat at the [Battle of Dandanaqan](/wiki/Battle_of_Dandanaqan "Battle of Dandanaqan") in 1040, he lost all the Ghaznavid lands in [Persia](/wiki/Persia "Persia") and Central Asia to the Seljuks, plunging the realm into a "time of troubles".[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth2006-13)[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAmirsoleimani1999243-45)[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpuler19911051-46) His last act was to collect all his treasures from his forts in hope of assembling an army and ruling from India, but his own forces plundered the wealth and he proclaimed his blind brother as king again. The two brothers now exchanged positions: Mohammed was elevated from prison to the throne, while Mas'ud was consigned to a dungeon after a reign of ten years and was assassinated in 1040. Mas'ud's son, Madood, was governor of Balkh, and in 1040, after hearing of his father's death, he came to Ghazni to claim his kingdom. He fought with the sons of the blind Mohammed and was victorious. However, the empire soon disintegrated and most kings did not submit to Madood. In a span of nine years, four more kings claimed the throne of Ghazni.

### Ibrahim

In 1058, Mas'ud's son [Ibrahim](/wiki/Ibrahim_of_Ghazna "Ibrahim of Ghazna"), a great calligrapher who wrote the Koran with his own pen, became king. Ibrahim re-established a truncated empire on a firmer basis by arriving at a peace agreement with the Seljuks and a restoration of cultural and political linkages.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth2006-13) Under Ibrahim and his successors the empire enjoyed a period of sustained tranquility. Shorn of its western land, it was increasingly sustained by riches accrued from raids across Northern India, where it faced stiff resistance from Indian rulers such as the [Paramara](/wiki/Paramara_dynasty "Paramara dynasty") of [Malwa](/wiki/Malwa "Malwa") and the [Gahadvala](/wiki/Gahadvala "Gahadvala") of [Kannauj](/wiki/Kannauj "Kannauj").[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth2006-13) He ruled until 1098.

### Mas'ud III

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Bahram-Shah_of_Ghazna_%28Kalila_and_Dimna%2C_folio_6a%29_end_of_13th_century%2C_Topkapi_H.363.jpg/250px-Bahram-Shah_of_Ghazna_%28Kalila_and_Dimna%2C_folio_6a%29_end_of_13th_century%2C_Topkapi_H.363.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bahram-Shah_of_Ghazna_(Kalila_and_Dimna,_folio_6a)_end_of_13th_century,_Topkapi_H.363.jpg)

Portrait of Ghaznavid ruler [Bahrām Shāh](/wiki/Bahram-Shah_of_Ghazna "Bahram-Shah of Ghazna") (d. 1152), (*[Kalila and Dimna](/wiki/Kalila_and_Dimna "Kalila and Dimna")*, folio 6a, end of 13th century, Topkapi H.363).[[43]](#cite_note-47)

[Mas'ud III](/wiki/Mas%27ud_III_of_Ghazni "Mas'ud III of Ghazni") became king for sixteen years, with no major event in his lifetime, prologing the period of peace established by his predecessor Ibrahim.[[44]](#cite_note-48)

Mas'ud built the [Palace of Sultan Mas'ud III](/wiki/Palace_of_Sultan_Mas%27ud_III "Palace of Sultan Mas'ud III") and one of the [Ghazni Minarets](/wiki/Ghazni_Minarets "Ghazni Minarets"). Signs of weakness in the state became apparent when he died in 1115, with internal strife between his sons ending with the ascension of Sultan [Bahram Shah](/wiki/Bahram-Shah_of_Ghazna "Bahram-Shah of Ghazna") as a Seljuk vassal.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth2006-13) Bahram Shah defeated his brother [Arslan](/wiki/Arslan_ibn_Mas%27ud "Arslan ibn Mas'ud") for the throne at the [Battle of Ghazni](/wiki/Battle_of_Ghazni_(1117) "Battle of Ghazni (1117)") in 1117.

### Sultan Bahram Shah

[Sultan Bahram Shah](/wiki/Bahram-Shah_of_Ghazna "Bahram-Shah of Ghazna") was the last Ghaznavid King, ruling [Ghazni](/wiki/Ghazna "Ghazna"), the first and main Ghaznavid capital, for thirty-five years. In 1148 he was [defeated in Ghazni](/wiki/Battle_of_Ghazni_(1148) "Battle of Ghazni (1148)") by [Sayf al-Din Suri](/wiki/Sayf_al-Din_Suri "Sayf al-Din Suri"), but he recaptured the capital the next year. [Ala al-Din Husayn](/wiki/Ala_al-Din_Husayn "Ala al-Din Husayn"), a [Ghorid](/wiki/Ghorid "Ghorid") King, [conquered the city](/wiki/Battle_of_Ghazni_(1151) "Battle of Ghazni (1151)") in 1151, in revenge for his brother Kutubbuddin's death, who was son-in-law of the king but was publicly punished and killed for a minor offence. Ala al-Din Husayn then razed the city, burning it for 7 days, after which he became known as *"Jahānsuz"* (*World Burner*). Ghazni was restored to the Ghaznavids by the intervention of the Seljuks, who came to the aid of Bahram.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth2006-13) Ghaznavid struggles with the Ghurids continued in subsequent years as they nibbled away at Ghaznavid territory, and Ghazni and [Zabulistan](/wiki/Zabulistan "Zabulistan") were lost to a group of [Oghuz Turks](/wiki/Oghuz_Turks "Oghuz Turks") before being captured by the Ghurids.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth2006-13) [Ghazni](/wiki/Ghazni "Ghazni") fell to the Ghurids around 1170.[[45]](#cite_note-CEB299-49)[[46]](#cite_note-50)

The last Ghaznavid king [Khusrau Malik](/wiki/Khusrau_Malik "Khusrau Malik") had his capital in [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"), [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab "Punjab"), until the [Ghurid](/wiki/Ghurid_Empire "Ghurid Empire") invasion of the subcontinent.[[47]](#cite_note-51)[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchwartzberg197832,_146-52)

After the fall of Ghazni in 1163, the Ghaznavids established themselves in [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"), their regional capital for Indian territories since its conquest by Mahmud of Ghazni, which became the new capital of the Late Ghaznavids.[[45]](#cite_note-CEB299-49) Ghaznavid power in northwestern India continued until the Ghurid conquest of [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore") by [Muhammad of Ghor](/wiki/Muhammad_of_Ghor "Muhammad of Ghor") in 1186, [deposing the last Ghaznavid ruler Khusrau Malik](/wiki/Siege_of_Lahore_(1186) "Siege of Lahore (1186)").[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth2006-13) Both [Khusrau Malik](/wiki/Khusrau_Malik "Khusrau Malik") and his son were imprisoned and summarily executed in [Firozkoh](/wiki/Firozkoh "Firozkoh") in 1191, extinguishing the Ghaznavid lineage.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth1977131-53)

## Military and tactics

The core of the Ghaznavid army was primarily made up of Turks,[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWink2002114-54) as well as thousands of native [Afghans](/wiki/Afghans "Afghans") who were trained and assembled from the area south of the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush") in what is now Afghanistan.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoutsma1987151-55) During the rule of Sultan Mahmud, a new, larger military training center was established in Bost (now [Lashkar Gah](/wiki/Lashkar_Gah "Lashkar Gah")). This area was known for [blacksmiths](/wiki/Blacksmith "Blacksmith") where war weapons were made. After capturing and conquering the [Punjab region](/wiki/Punjab_region "Punjab region"), the Ghaznavids began to employ [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus") in their army.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoy201624-56)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Ghaznavid_soldiers%2C_circa_1100_%28Cleveland_Museum_of_Art%2C_1980.179%29.jpg/250px-Ghaznavid_soldiers%2C_circa_1100_%28Cleveland_Museum_of_Art%2C_1980.179%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ghaznavid_soldiers,_circa_1100_(Cleveland_Museum_of_Art,_1980.179).jpg)

Ghaznavid soldiers, circa 1100 (Cleveland Museum of Art, 1980.179)

The Indian soldiers, whom [Romila Thapar](/wiki/Romila_Thapar "Romila Thapar") presumed to be [Hindus](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu"), were one of the components of the army with their commander called *[sipahsalar](/wiki/Sipahsalar "Sipahsalar")-i-Hinduwan* and lived in their own quarter of Ghazna practicing their own religion. Indian soldiers under their commander Suvendhray remained loyal to Mahmud. They were also used against a Turkic rebel, with the command given to a Hindu named Tilak according to [Baihaki](/wiki/Abu%27l-Fadl_Bayhaqi "Abu'l-Fadl Bayhaqi").[[53]](#cite_note-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Confronted_warriors%2C_carved_marble_relief_from_Ghazna%2C_Ghaznawid%2C_c.1100_AD_%28David_Collection%2C_inv._22-1989%2C_Copenhagen%2C_Denmark%29.jpg/250px-Confronted_warriors%2C_carved_marble_relief_from_Ghazna%2C_Ghaznawid%2C_c.1100_AD_%28David_Collection%2C_inv._22-1989%2C_Copenhagen%2C_Denmark%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Confronted_warriors,_carved_marble_relief_from_Ghazna,_Ghaznawid,_c.1100_AD_(David_Collection,_inv._22-1989,_Copenhagen,_Denmark).jpg)

Confronted warriors. Drawing from carved marble relief from Ghazna, Ghaznawid, c.1100 CE. David Collection, inv. 22/1989, Copenhagen, Denmark.[[54]](#cite_note-58)

Like the other dynasties that rose out of the remains of the [Abbasid Caliphate](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphate "Abbasid Caliphate"), the Ghaznavid administrative traditions and military practice came from the Abbasids. The [Arabian horses](/wiki/Arabian_horse "Arabian horse"), at least in the earliest campaign, were still substantial in Ghaznavid military incursions, especially in dashing raids deep into hostile territory. There is a record of '6000 Arab horse' being sent against king Anandapala in 1008, and evidence of this Arabian cavalry persists until 1118 under the Ghaznavid governor in [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore").[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth1963111-112-59)

Due to their access to the [Indus-Ganges plains](/wiki/Indo-Gangetic_Plain "Indo-Gangetic Plain"), the Ghaznavids, during the 11th and 12th centuries, developed the first Muslim army to use [war elephants](/wiki/War_elephants "War elephants") in battle.[[56]](#cite_note-60) The elephants were protected by [armour plating](/wiki/Armour_plating "Armour plating") on their fronts. The use of these elephants was a foreign weapon in other regions that the Ghaznavids fought in, particularly in Central Asia.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELewis1992205-61)

## State and culture

See also: [List of Ghaznavid Viziers](/wiki/List_of_Ghaznavid_Viziers "List of Ghaznavid Viziers")

Although the dynasty was of [Central Asian](/wiki/Central_Asian "Central Asian") Turkic origin, it was thoroughly [Persianised](/wiki/Persianised "Persianised") in terms of language, culture, literature and habits[[e]](#cite_note-63)[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZiad2006294-62)[[f]](#cite_note-65)[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisami1999143-64) and has been regarded as a "Persian dynasty".[[g]](#cite_note-67)

According to [Clifford Edmund Bosworth](/wiki/Clifford_Edmund_Bosworth "Clifford Edmund Bosworth"):

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Ghaznavid_ruler_portrait%2C_circa_1100_%28Cleveland_Museum_of_Art%2C_1980.179%29.jpg/250px-Ghaznavid_ruler_portrait%2C_circa_1100_%28Cleveland_Museum_of_Art%2C_1980.179%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ghaznavid_ruler_portrait,_circa_1100_(Cleveland_Museum_of_Art,_1980.179).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Ghaznavid_salver%2C_circa_1100_%28Cleveland_Museum_of_Art%2C_1980.179%29.jpg/250px-Ghaznavid_salver%2C_circa_1100_%28Cleveland_Museum_of_Art%2C_1980.179%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ghaznavid_salver,_circa_1100_(Cleveland_Museum_of_Art,_1980.179).jpg)

Ghaznavid Sultan and his court, on a brass [salver](/wiki/Salver "Salver") plate. Dated circa 1100, Ghaznavid period, Afghanistan, probably [Herat](/wiki/Herat "Herat") or [Ghazni](/wiki/Ghazni "Ghazni"). [Cleveland Museum of Art](/wiki/Cleveland_Museum_of_Art "Cleveland Museum of Art").[[61]](#cite_note-CMOA-68)[[62]](#cite_note-69) The sultan is seated in the traditional cross-legged Turkish posture, and "the round faces and almond eyes of the figures reflect the Turkish facial type of that period".[[63]](#cite_note-70) Inscriptions in Arabic.[[61]](#cite_note-CMOA-68)

> The Ghaznavid sultans were ethnically [Turkish](/wiki/Turkic_peoples "Turkic peoples"), but the sources, all in [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") or [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), do not allow us to estimate the persistence of Turkish practices and ways of thought amongst them. Yet given the fact that the essential basis of the Ghaznavids' military support always remained their Turkish soldiery, there must always have been a need to stay attuned to their troops' needs and aspirations; also, there are indications of the persistence of some Turkish literary culture under the early Ghaznavids (Köprülüzade, pp. 56–57). The sources do make it clear, however, that the sultans' exercise of political power and the administrative apparatus which gave it shape came very speedily to be within the Perso-Islamic tradition of statecraft and monarchical rule, with the ruler as a distant figure, buttressed by divine favor, ruling over a mass of traders, artisans, peasants, etc., whose prime duty was obedience in all respects but above all in the payment of taxes. The fact that the personnel of the [bureaucracy](/wiki/Bureaucracy "Bureaucracy") which directed the day-to-day running of the state, and which raised the revenue to support the sultans' life-style and to finance the professional army, were [Persians](/wiki/Persian_people "Persian people") who carried on the administrative traditions of the Samanids, only strengthened this conception of secular power.[[64]](#cite_note-71)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Anthropomorphic_relief_from_Ghazni._Kabul_Museum_%28inv._58.2.1%29.jpg/500px-Anthropomorphic_relief_from_Ghazni._Kabul_Museum_%28inv._58.2.1%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anthropomorphic_relief_from_Ghazni._Kabul_Museum_(inv._58.2.1).jpg)

Frieze from Ghazni, depicting Ghanavids with round faces and prominent cheekbones, wearing Turkic clothes. 11th-12th century, [Kabul Museum](/wiki/Kabul_Museum "Kabul Museum") (inv. 58.2.1).[[65]](#cite_note-72) The inscription is in Persian.[[66]](#cite_note-73)

[Persianisation](/wiki/Persianization "Persianization") of the state apparatus was accompanied by the Persianisation of high culture at the Ghaznavid court... The level of literary creativity was just as high under Ebrāhīm and his successors up to Bahrāmšāh, with such poets as Abu’l-Faraj Rūnī, Sanāʾī, ʿOṯmān Moḵtārī, Masʿūd-e Saʿd-e Salmān, and Sayyed Ḥasan Ḡaznavī.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth197775-77,_107-110-74) We know from the biographical dictionaries of poets (taḏkera-ye šoʿarā) that the court in Lahore of Ḵosrow Malek had an array of fine poets, none of whose dīvāns has unfortunately survived, and the translator into elegant Persian prose of Ebn Moqaffaʿ’s Kalīla wa Demna, namely Abu’l-Maʿālī Naṣr-Allāh b. Moḥammad, served the sultan for a while as his chief secretary.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth1977127-128-75) The Ghaznavids thus present the phenomenon of a dynasty of Turkish slave origin which became culturally Persianised to a perceptibly higher degree than other contemporary dynasties of Turkish origin such as [Saljuqs](/wiki/Seljuq_Empire "Seljuq Empire") and [Qarakhanids](/wiki/Kara-Khanid_Khanate "Kara-Khanid Khanate").[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth2006-13)

Persian literary culture enjoyed a renaissance under the Ghaznavids during the 11th century.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth196844-76)[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharlet201146-77)[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowson1998251-78) The Ghaznavid court was so renowned for its support of Persian literature that the poet [Farrukhi](/wiki/Farrukhi_Sistani "Farrukhi Sistani") traveled from his home province to work for them.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharlet201127-79) The poet Unsuri's short collection of poetry was dedicated to [Sultan Mahmud](/wiki/Mahmud_of_Ghazni "Mahmud of Ghazni") and his brothers Nasr and Yaqub.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESharlet201152-80) Another poet of the Ghaznavid court, [Manuchehri](/wiki/Manuchehri "Manuchehri"), wrote numerous poems about the merits of drinking wine.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYarshater196044-81)

Sultan Mahmud, modelling the Samanid Bukhara as a cultural center, made Ghazni into a center of learning, inviting Ferdowsi and al-Biruni. He even attempted to persuade [Avicenna](/wiki/Avicenna "Avicenna"), but was refused.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoonerHanaway2012284-82) Mahmud preferred that his fame and glory be publicized in Persian and hundreds of poets assembled at his court.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENotghiSabri-Tabrizi1994244-83) He brought whole libraries from Rayy and Isfahan to Ghazni and even demanded that the Khwarizmshah court send its men of learning to Ghazni.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth1963132-84) Due to his invasion of Rayy and Isfahan, Persian literary production was inaugurated in [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan_(Iran) "Azerbaijan (Iran)") and [Iraq](/wiki/Persian_Iraq "Persian Iraq").[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAhmadi2004146-85)

The Ghaznavids continued to develop historical writing in Persian that had been initiated by their predecessors, the [Samanid Empire](/wiki/Samanid_Empire "Samanid Empire").[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisami1993247-86) The historian [Abu'l-Fadl Bayhaqi](/wiki/Abu%27l-Fadl_Bayhaqi "Abu'l-Fadl Bayhaqi")'s *[Tarikh-e Beyhaqi](/wiki/Tarikh-e_Beyhaqi "Tarikh-e Beyhaqi")*, written in the latter half of the 11th century, is an example.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPoliakova1984241-87)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Ghaznavid_court_scene%2C_circa_1100_%28Cleveland_Museum_of_Art%2C_1980.179%29.jpg/500px-Ghaznavid_court_scene%2C_circa_1100_%28Cleveland_Museum_of_Art%2C_1980.179%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ghaznavid_court_scene,_circa_1100_(Cleveland_Museum_of_Art,_1980.179).jpg)

Ghaznavid court scene, circa 1100 (Cleveland Museum of Art, 1980.179)

Although the Ghaznavids were Turkic and their military leaders were generally of the same stock,[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth19634"In_this_book_I_have_discussed_the_Ghaznavids_as_a_Turkish_dynasty,_of_slave_origin,_who_established_themselves_on_the_eastern_margins_of_the_Iranian_world_[...]_these_Turkish_condotierri_became_rulers_of_what_was,_at_Mahmud's_death_in_1030,_the_most_extensive_empire_known_in_the_eastern_Islamic_world,_since_the_dismemberment_of_the_Abassid_Caliphate"-88) as a result of the original involvement of [Sebuktigin](/wiki/Sebuktigin "Sebuktigin") and [Mahmud of Ghazni](/wiki/Mahmud_of_Ghazni "Mahmud of Ghazni") in Samanid affairs and in the Samanid cultural environment, the dynasty became thoroughly Persianized, so that in practice one cannot consider their rule over Iran one of foreign domination. They also copied their administrative system from the Samanids.[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth196836-89) In terms of cultural championship and the support of Persian poets, they were more Persian than their ethnically-Iranian rivals, the [Buyid dynasty](/wiki/Buyid_dynasty "Buyid dynasty"), whose support of Arabic letters in preference to Persian is well known.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYarshater2008-90)

The 16th century Persian historian, [Firishta](/wiki/Firishta "Firishta"), records Sabuktigin's [genealogy](/wiki/Genealogy "Genealogy") as descended from the [Sasanian kings](/wiki/Sasanian_Empire "Sasanian Empire"): "Subooktu-geen, the son of Jookan, the son of Kuzil-Hukum, the son of Kuzil-Arslan, the son of Ferooz, the son of [Yezdijird](/wiki/Yazdegerd_III "Yazdegerd III"), king of Persia." However, modern historians believe this was an attempt to connect himself with the history of old Persia.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth196840-91)

Historian Bosworth explains: "In fact with the adoption of Persian administrative and cultural ways the Ghaznavids threw off their original Turkish steppe background and became largely integrated with the Perso-Islamic tradition."[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth1996297-92) As a result, [Ghazni](/wiki/Ghazni "Ghazni") developed into a great centre of Arabic learning.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth1963134-4)

With Sultan Mahmud's invasions of [North India](/wiki/North_India "North India"), Persian culture was established at Lahore, which later produced the famous poet, [Masud Sa'd Salman](/wiki/Masud_Sa%27d_Salman "Masud Sa'd Salman").[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZiad2006294-62) Lahore, under Ghaznavid rule in the 11th century, attracted Persian scholars from Khorasan, India and Central Asia and became a major Persian cultural centre.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlamNaliniGaborieau200024-93)[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpoonerHanaway2012284-82) One of the most significant early works on Sufism, the Kashf al-mahjub, was written in Lahore by Abu al-Hasan Hujwiri al-Ghaznawi.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhanbaghi2016201-94) It was also during Mahmud's reign that Ghaznavid coinage began to have bilingual legends consisting of Arabic and Devanagari script.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth196344-25) The entire range of Persianate institutions and customs that would come to characterize the political economy of most of India would be implemented by the later Ghaznavids.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEaton201935-95)

The Persian culture established by the Ghaznavids in Ghazna and Eastern Afghanistan survived the Ghurid invasion in the 12th century and endured until the invasion of the Mongols.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBosworth196839-96)

## Art and Architecture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Minaret_of_Mas%27ud_III_in_1846.jpg/250px-Minaret_of_Mas%27ud_III_in_1846.jpg)](/wiki/File:Minaret_of_Mas%27ud_III_in_1846.jpg)

[Mas'ud III](/wiki/Mas%27ud_III_of_Ghazni "Mas'ud III of Ghazni")'s [minaret in Ghazni](/wiki/Ghazni_minaret "Ghazni minaret") was at least 44 meters tall, before its top half crumbled in 1902 due to an earthquake. It was built between 1099 and 1115 CE. It stood next to the [Palace of Sultan Mas'ud III](/wiki/Palace_of_Sultan_Mas%27ud_III "Palace of Sultan Mas'ud III").[[90]](#cite_note-RPW-97)

Main articles: [Palace of Sultan Mas'ud III](/wiki/Palace_of_Sultan_Mas%27ud_III "Palace of Sultan Mas'ud III"), [Lashkari Bazar](/wiki/Lashkari_Bazar "Lashkari Bazar"), and [Ghazni Minarets](/wiki/Ghazni_Minarets "Ghazni Minarets")

During the Ghaznavid era, artistic production was at its height, due to increased patronage from elites and the economic benefits of spoils gained from increasing raids and forced tributes from India. The height of which being the attack on the [Somnath Temple](/wiki/Somnath_temple "Somnath temple") in Kathiawar peninsula, resulting in a large hoard of treasure being taken into the empire. Ghaznavid art focused on adapting ancient artistic techniques to new materials and mediums, especially in the etching of precious metals, to leave a lasting impression on the Islamic art world.[[91]](#cite_note-98) In the plastic arts, bronze-works appear to be influenced by earlier Samanid pieces, yet are unique enough that early versions of trademarks are visible on some pieces. Two bronze ewers, analyzed by Eva Baer, denote an influence from more contemporary ceramics in their shape and construction, while bearing both archaic methods of hatching and novel "oval... with a triangular base" designs, along with the artist’s signatures.[[92]](#cite_note-99)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/MNAO-GhazniPalMasudIII-Lastra1.jpg/250px-MNAO-GhazniPalMasudIII-Lastra1.jpg)](/wiki/File:MNAO-GhazniPalMasudIII-Lastra1.jpg)

Marble wall border, [Palace of Sultan Mas'ud III](/wiki/Palace_of_Sultan_Mas%27ud_III "Palace of Sultan Mas'ud III"), Ghazni, Afghanistan, 12th century CE.

Other works such as the garden murals added by [Mas'ud I](/wiki/Mas%CA%BDud_I "Masʽud I") in the [Herat](/wiki/Herat "Herat") palace complex had representational depictions including lewd subject matter of nude figures in "convivial" scenes.[[93]](#cite_note-100) The capital, Ghazni, was also considered a center for lyrical poetry in the eastern Islamic world due to poets being able to form genres and styles over a long period of time.[[94]](#cite_note-101)

Ghaznavid architecture was especially able to flourish, specifically in marble reliefs with geometric, vegetal, and epigraphic designs.[[95]](#cite_note-102) Due to their capital, Ghazni, being near an important trade route it resulted in influences from all aver the region. Near the Palace of Mas'ud III there is a minaret that was constructed of baked and unbaked pressed clay bricks and it is surrounded by a courtyard with four iwans. Excavations at this sites courtyard have uncovered numerous carved marble panels that feature trefoil arches, scrollwork, and inscriptions in Persian and Arabic.[[96]](#cite_note-:0-103) One of the more celebrated works is a marble panel from the courtyard of Sultan Mas'ud III's palace, now in the Brooklyn Museum, which has a Kufic script above intricate vegetal motifs.[[97]](#cite_note-104) The use of marble instead of more common stucco or brick along with detailed calligraphy reflect both the Ghaznavids' wealth and their artistry.[[96]](#cite_note-:0-103)

The Ghaznavids and other polities in continental Asia c. 1100

At its height, the Ghaznavid empire grew from the Oxus to the Indus Valley and was ruled from 977 to 1186. The history of the empire, [Tarikh Yamini](/wiki/Tarikh_Yamini "Tarikh Yamini"), was written by [Muhammad ibn Abd al-Jabbar al-Utbi](/wiki/Muhammad_ibn_Abd_al-Jabbar_al-Utbi "Muhammad ibn Abd al-Jabbar al-Utbi"), who documented the Ghaznavid's achievements, including regaining lost territory from their rivals, the [Kara-Khanids](/wiki/Kara-Khanid_Khanate "Kara-Khanid Khanate"), in present-day Iran and Afghanistan.[[98]](#cite_note-105)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/MassudOfGhazniCoin.jpg/250px-MassudOfGhazniCoin.jpg)](/wiki/File:MassudOfGhazniCoin.jpg)

Coinage of [Mas'ud I of Ghazni](/wiki/Mas%27ud_I_of_Ghazni "Mas'ud I of Ghazni") (1030–1041), derived from [Hindu Shahi](/wiki/Hindu_Shahi "Hindu Shahi") designs, with the name of Mas'ud ([Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"): مسعود) around the head of the horserider.

In addition to the wealth accumulated through raiding Indian cities, and exacting tribute from Indian [rajas](/wiki/Raja "Raja"), the Ghaznavids also benefited from their position as an intermediary along the trade routes between China and the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean"). The Ghaznavid rulers are generally credited with spreading Islam into the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent").

They were, however, unable to hold power for long and by 1040 the [Seljuk Empire](/wiki/Seljuk_Empire "Seljuk Empire") had taken over their [Persian](/wiki/Persian_Empire "Persian Empire") domains and a century later the [Ghurids](/wiki/Ghurid_dynasty "Ghurid dynasty") took over their remaining sub-continental lands.

The Ghaznavid conquests facilitated the beginning of the Turko-Afghan period into India, which would be further conducted by the [Ghurids](/wiki/Ghurid_dynasty "Ghurid dynasty") until the Turko-Afghans successfully established themselves in the [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate").[[99]](#cite_note-106)[[100]](#cite_note-107)

At the same time, the official language was Persian, the court literature was in Persian, and most of the chancellors, ministers, and mandarins were Persian speakers of the highest learning and ability.*"[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKatouzian2003128-1)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-b_10-0)** The Ghaznavids also claimed ancestry from the last [Sasanian](/wiki/Sasanian "Sasanian") Shah, [Yazdgerd III](/wiki/Yazdgerd_III "Yazdgerd III"),[[7]](#cite_note-8) but this was "a fictitious genealogy" they themselves had promulgated.[[8]](#cite_note-9)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-19)** The Ghaznavids were a dynasty of Turkic slave-soldiers...[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeviSela201083-18)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-29)** Kaushik Roy states Turkic nobles at Balkh chose Ismail as Emir.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoy201588-28)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-63)** "The Ghaznavids inherited Samanid administrative, political, and cultural traditions and laid the foundations for a Persianate state in northern India. ..."[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZiad2006294-62)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-65)** Nizam al-Mulk also attempted to organise the Saljuq administration according to the Persianate Ghaznavid model.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMeisami1999143-64)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-67)** Firdawsi was writing his Shah-nama. One of the effects of the renaissance of the Persian spirit
   evoked by this work was that the Ghaznavids were also persianized and thereby became a Persian dynasty"[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpuler1970147-66)
